questions.question: Your shop runs only a hydrophilic post-emulsified fluorescent line, and you want the ASTM practice written specifically for that single process. You reach for:
questions.explanation: E1210 governs the hydrophilic post-emulsification fluorescent process (Method D). E1208 is the lipophilic practice, E1209 the water-washable practice, and E165 the general-industry umbrella.

questions.question: Within ASME Section V, Article 6, the adopted general-industry penetrant practice appears under the designation:
questions.explanation: ASME adopts ASTM E165 for code work as SE-165; Article 6 builds its penetrant methodology on that adopted practice.

questions.question: ASTM E1417 descends from a superseded military penetrant standard and is most likely to be invoked on which job?
options: Structural steel handrails for a warehouse
questions.explanation: E1417 is the aerospace and defense practice - QPL materials per AMS 2644 and mandatory process-control checks - so engine, airframe, and military hardware is its territory.

questions.question: Typical procedure values for a Method A water wash are:
options: A fine mist at any pressure with 150 °F water, washed for a fixed five minutes
A coarse spray, commonly no more than about 40 psi with roughly 50–100 °F water, stopped when background is gone
A solid stream at 100 psi with cold water, washed until the surface looks polished
Immersion in agitated hot water for ten minutes
questions.explanation: The wash is a coarse, low-pressure spray - commonly capped near 40 psi with moderate-temperature water - and it ends the moment background clears, because extra pressure, heat, or time strips flaw entrapments.

questions.question: The key operational difference between lipophilic (Method B) and hydrophilic (Method D) removal is:
options: Method B uses a water-based detergent while Method D uses an oil-based emulsifier
Method B requires a water pre-rinse while Method D forbids rinsing
Method B's emulsifier diffuses into the penetrant layer on a timed immersion contact, while Method D's detergent remover acts only at the surface after a water pre-rinse
The two methods differ only in penetrant color
questions.explanation: Lipophilic emulsifier is oil-based and works by timed diffusion into the penetrant; hydrophilic remover is a water-based detergent that scrubs only the surface layer after a pre-rinse - the basis of its superior control.

questions.question: A refractometer on a penetrant processing line is used to verify:
options: UV-A intensity at the examination surface
Hydrophilic remover concentration in the bath
questions.explanation: Hydrophilic remover is mixed with water (dip baths commonly around 5–35%), and its concentration is checked with a refractometer as routine process control; UV intensity takes a radiometer, not a refractometer.

questions.question: For a small-area solvent-removable check on a smooth machined surface, the developer form generally regarded as the most sensitive in common spot use is:
questions.explanation: Nonaqueous wet developer lays down a thin, uniform white film with strong blotting action, making it the most sensitive form in common spot use and the natural partner of Method C.

questions.question: During a turnaround, one nozzle weld high in a pipe rack needs PT. No water or power is available at the location. The expected call is:
options: Type I, Method A with a portable booth and water bowser
Type II, Method C aerosol kit with at least about 100 fc verified at the surface
Type I, Method D with a portable remover bath
Postpone until the weld can be cut out and brought to the shop
questions.explanation: A localized exam with no utilities is the defining Method C job: visible dye, solvent-dampened wipe removal, nonaqueous developer, and a verified white-light level at the surface.

questions.question: Which discontinuity is MOST at risk of being lost to over-washing on a water-washable line?
options: Subsurface porosity with no surface opening
questions.explanation: Shallow, broad entrapments give up their penetrant most easily under excess pressure, heat, or wash time. Deep tight cracks hold on longer - and porosity with no surface opening was never detectable by PT in the first place.

questions.question: A field crew wants to run fluorescent PT on an outdoor weld at midday "because fluorescent is more sensitive." The lighting constraint they are ignoring is:
options: White light must exceed 100 fc, which daylight cannot provide
UV-A must be kept below 1,000 µW/cm² in daylight
Fluorescent examination needs ambient white light held to about 2 fc with UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface - impossible at midday without blackout
questions.explanation: Type I viewing requires a near-dark surface (ambient roughly 2 fc or less) plus adequate UV-A intensity; open daylight swamps the fluorescence, so the field job belongs to Type II unless full blackout is rigged.
